input.content:
---
created: 2026-04-09
categories: [[research-source]]
source_type: multi-source-synthesis
quality_tier: 2
confidence: community-consensus
domain: [[knowledge-management]]
tags: [second-brain, RAG, AI-integration, conversation-extraction, obsidian]
processed: false
---

## Key Findings

### 1. Core Second Brain Methodologies in Obsidian

The dominant approaches people combine in Obsidian are **Zettelkasten** (atomic notes, liberal linking, emergent structure) and **Building a Second Brain / PARA** (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives). The Kepano/Steph Ango system — flat root, categories as properties, composable templates, rating 1-7 — is widely adopted by power users. Many practitioners fuse PARA's organizational framework with Zettelkasten's linking philosophy. (Source: [PARAZETTEL fusion guide](https://parazettel.com/articles/fusing-basb-zettelkasten-obsidian/), [Anjan on Medium](https://medium.com/@anjanj/the-ultimate-zettelkasten-system-how-i-built-a-second-brain-in-obsidian-95c29f89c6f7))

### 2. AI + Obsidian Conversation Integration

Two major open-source projects demonstrate conversation-to-vault pipelines built with Claude Code skills:

**eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain** — 25 skill commands. The `/obsidian-save` command extracts decisions, tasks, entities, and concepts from the current conversation and distributes each to the appropriate note type. A PostCompact hook fires a headless background agent (`obsidian-bg-agent.sh → claude -p`) that autonomously updates the vault while the user continues working. Four scheduled agents (morning/nightly/weekly/health) maintain the vault — nightly reconciles contradictions, synthesizes patterns, and heals orphan notes. Uses a `_CLAUDE.md` operating manual as the context system and progressive token loading (L0-L3) for scalable context budgets. (Source: [GitHub](https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain))

**huytieu/COG-second-brain** — 17 skills, self-evolving via a daily → weekly → monthly synthesis cycle. Raw braindumps get intelligently classified by domain, weekly analysis detects cross-domain patterns invisible in individual notes, monthly consolidation builds frameworks. Verification-first: 7-day freshness on news, sources required, confidence levels explicit. Processes 120+ braindumps with 95%+ source accuracy. (Source: [GitHub](https://github.com/huytieu/COG-second-brain), [DEV Community](https://dev.to/huy_tieu/i-finally-built-a-second-brain-that-i-actually-use-6th-attempt-4075))

### 3. Conversation-to-Knowledge Pipeline Architecture

The MindStudio architecture describes a **three-layer memory system**:
- **Working memory** — session-scoped context, auto-expires after 48 hours
- **Episodic memory** — timestamped records of each interaction (context, observations, patterns, links to vault notes)
- **Semantic memory** — distilled knowledge extracted from episodic records by a reflection cycle, NOT written during sessions

The **reflection/heartbeat cycle** (scheduled, e.g., daily at 6 AM):
1. Archives stale working memory → episodic
2. Reviews recent episodes for recurring themes
3. Extracts patterns → updates semantic notes
4. Creates daily briefing
5. Prunes semantic records unchanged for 90+ days

This creates the feedback loop: conversation → episodic record → pattern extraction → semantic update → informed future conversations. (Source: [MindStudio architecture guide](https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-second-brain-claude-code-obsidian-architecture))

### 4. RAG with Obsidian Vaults

**Smart Connections** (100K+ users) — Embeds vault notes locally, uses HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) for context lookup, lets you chat with your entire vault. Shows you exactly what context goes to the model before sending. Local-first, no API key required for embeddings. (Source: [GitHub](https://github.com/brianpetro/obsidian-smart-connections), [smartconnections.app](https://smartconnections.app))

**Smart Composer** — RAG-based vault search integrated into the editor. (Source: [GitHub wiki](https://github.com/glowingjade/obsidian-smart-composer/wiki/2.3-Vault-Search-(RAG)))

**ObsidianRAG** — External RAG system using LangGraph + Ollama for fully local/private querying. (Source: [GitHub](https://github.com/Vasallo94/ObsidianRAG))

**Copilot for Obsidian** — 100K+ users, most downloaded AI integration. (Source: [obsidiancopilot.com](https://www.obsidiancopilot.com/en))

Key pattern: all RAG solutions treat the vault as a vector-searchable knowledge base where the AI grounds answers in YOUR notes rather than general training data.

### 5. Learning-Specific Second Brain Patterns

- **Spaced repetition integration** — Obsidian's Spaced Repetition plugin generates flashcards from notes. Combined with Zettelkasten, you study atomic concepts rather than monolithic documents.
- **Progressive summarization** — Tiago Forte's technique: highlight the best passages, then bold the best highlights, creating layers of distillation. Maps naturally to Obsidian's formatting.
- **Knowledge classification** — COG's verification tiers (solid/partial/gap) mirror learning science's known-unknown-misconception framework.
- **Goal-driven paths** — Both COG and the MindStudio architecture organize knowledge around goals/projects, not just topics. Notes link to goals, creating "learning paths" through the graph.

## Relevance

This research directly informs the evolution of the MyAgent learning agent. The current architecture (research → raw → wiki → tutor) is a one-way pipeline. The second brain pattern adds feedback loops: conversations become episodic records, reflection cycles extract patterns, and the vault grows from every interaction — not just explicit research sessions.

## Key Takeaway for MyAgent

The missing piece is **conversation extraction + reflection**. The agent currently discards all tutoring conversations. Adding a conversation → vault pipeline would mean every time the tutor explains gain staging, the interaction itself gets deposited as an episodic note, the reflection cycle notices "user keeps asking about gain staging → EQ interaction," and a semantic note connecting those concepts gets auto-generated.

content.text:
Here's the synthesis of what I found and what it means for your agent:

---

## Research Summary: Obsidian Second Brain + Conversation Integration

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**Your agent is already 80% of a second brain system.** The Zettelkasten vault, research-first pipeline, equipment-specific personalization, and Kepano-style structure are solid. What's missing is the **feedback loop** — the part where conversations themselves feed knowledge back into the vault.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

### What the community is doing (the 3 most relevant projects)

**1. [eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain](https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain)** — 25 Claude Code skills. The key innovation: `/obsidian-save` extracts decisions, tasks, entities, and concepts from the current conversation and distributes them into the vault. A PostCompact hook runs a headless background agent that updates the vault autonomously. Scheduled agents (morning/nightly/weekly) reconcile contradictions, synthesize patterns, and heal orphan notes.

**2. [huytieu/COG-second-brain](https://github.com/huytieu/COG-second-brain)** — Self-evolving via a daily → weekly → monthly synthesis cycle. Raw braindumps get classified, weekly analysis detects cross-domain patterns, monthly consolidation builds frameworks. Verification-first: sources required, confidence levels, 7-day freshness.

**3. [MindStudio three-layer memory architecture](https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-second-brain-claude-code-obsidian-architecture)** — Working memory (session) → Episodic memory (timestamped interaction records) → Semantic memory (distilled knowledge). A "heartbeat" reflection cycle runs daily to extract patterns from episodes and update semantic notes. The key rule: semantic memory is written by the reflection cycle, never during live sessions.

### What this means for your agent — Recommended Next Steps

**Tier 1: Do these for the homework demo**
1. **Run the agent end-to-end** — Use `/session-router` to go through onboarding as a new user (profile is now blank). This proves the skill chain works.
2. **Run `/research-and-ingest`** on 2-3 inbox topics to populate `vault/raw/` with real sourced content.
3. **Run `/wiki-builder`** to synthesize those into wiki notes — this is what makes the tutor demo work (teaching from grounded sources, not general knowledge).

**Tier 2: Conversation extraction (the second brain evolution)**
4. **Add a `conversation-log` skill** — After a tutoring session, extract what was taught, what the user struggled with, what clicked, and deposit it as an episodic note in `vault/daily/` or a new `vault/episodes/` folder. This is what eugeniughelbur's `/obsidian-save` does.
5. **Add a reflection/synthesis cycle** — A skill (or scheduled agent) that reads recent episode notes and surfaces patterns: "User keeps asking about EQ after gain staging → these topics are linked in their mental model" → auto-generates a connection note or updates the learning path.

**Tier 3: Full second brain (post-homework)**
6. **Three-layer memory** — Working (session context) → Episodic (conversation records) → Semantic (distilled knowledge). The reflection heartbeat cycle connects them.
7. **RAG integration** — Use Smart Connections or similar to let the tutor semantically search the vault rather than relying on index-file navigation.
8. **Scheduled agents** — Morning briefing ("here's what to study today based on your momentum"), nightly reconciliation (process inbox, heal orphan links).

Sources:
- [eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain](https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain)
- [huytieu/COG-second-brain](https://github.com/huytieu/COG-second-brain)
- [MindStudio: AI Second Brain Architecture](https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-second-brain-claude-code-obsidian-architecture)
- [Smart Connections Plugin](https://github.com/brianpetro/obsidian-smart-connections)
- [NxCode: Obsidian AI Second Brain Guide 2026](https://www.nxcode.io/resources/news/obsidian-ai-second-brain-complete-guide-2026)
- [WhyTryAI: Claude Code + Obsidian](https://www.whytryai.com/p/claude-code-obsidian)
- [PARAZETTEL: Fusing BASB and Zettelkasten](https://parazettel.com/articles/fusing-basb-zettelkasten-obsidian/)

The raw research is deposited in `vault/raw/obsidian-second-brain-ai-integration-research-2026-04-09.md` and indexed in `_raw-index.md`. Want me to start on any of the Tier 1 or Tier 2 items?

content.content:
# Second Brain: LLM-Maintained Knowledge Base

## How It Works

Second Brain is a system where users feed raw source material (articles, papers, notes, transcripts) into a `raw/` folder. An LLM then processes this content to create a structured wiki with cross-references and an index, all browsable in Obsidian. As the creator frames it: "The LLM is the librarian. You're the curator."

## Architecture

The vault structure consists of:
- **raw/** — input inbox for sources and assets
- **wiki/** — organized into sources, entities, concepts, synthesis pages, plus index.md and operation log
- **output/** — generated reports and artifacts
- **Agent config** — varies by AI platform (CLAUDE.md, etc.)

## Foundation & Comparison

Yes, it's explicitly built on Andrej Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern. The README doesn't mention eugeniushelbr or COG, so direct comparisons aren't available in this source material.

## Core Skills/Features

Four installed skills provide functionality:

| Skill | Function |
|-------|----------|
| `/second-brain` | Guided vault setup wizard |
| `/second-brain-ingest` | Compile sources into wiki pages |
| `/second-brain-query` | Search and ask questions |
| `/second-brain-lint` | Health-check and repair |

Optional tools include summarize, qmd (fast markdown search), and agent-browser.

## Engagement Metrics

**79 stars** on GitHub, with 4 forks and 3 watchers at time of capture.

content.content:
# Claude Code Skill: Obsidian Second Brain - Complete Analysis

## Overview
This is a Claude Code skill that transforms Obsidian vaults into "living second brains" through autonomous AI management, featuring 24+ commands, scheduled agents, and intelligent synthesis capabilities.

## The 24 Commands (Three Layers)

### Layer 1: Operations (20 Commands) - Memory Management
- **`/obsidian-save`**: Extracts decisions, tasks, people, and ideas from conversations into appropriate vault notes
- **`/obsidian-ingest`**: Processes URLs, PDFs, audio files, or images; rewrites 5-15 existing pages with new context
- **`/obsidian-synthesize`**: Auto-discovers cross-source patterns and generates synthesis pages
- **`/obsidian-reconcile`**: Identifies and resolves contradictions within the vault
- **`/obsidian-export`**: Generates clean JSON/markdown snapshots for other AI tools
- **`/obsidian-daily`**: Creates/updates daily notes with calendar events and overdue tasks
- **`/obsidian-log`**: Records work sessions with vault-wide linking
- **`/obsidian-task`**: Adds tasks to appropriate boards with priority/due dates
- **`/obsidian-person`**: Creates or updates person entity notes
- **`/obsidian-decide`**: Logs decisions to project notes
- **`/obsidian-capture`**: Zero-friction idea capture
- **`/obsidian-find`**: Context-aware smart search
- **`/obsidian-recap`**: Generates summaries for days, weeks, or months
- **`/obsidian-review`**: Structured review sessions
- **`/obsidian-board`**: Kanban board viewing and updates
- **`/obsidian-project`**: Creates project notes with board and daily note links
- **`/obsidian-health`**: Audits vault for contradictions, gaps, stale claims, orphaned notes
- **`/obsidian-adr`**: Architecture Decision Records tracking
- **`/obsidian-visualize`**: Generates visual canvas maps showing vault topology
- **`/obsidian-init`**: Bootstraps vault with `_CLAUDE.md`, `index.md`, `log.md`

### Layer 2: Thinking Tools (4 Commands) - Collaborative Intelligence
- **`/obsidian-challenge`**: Uses your vault history to argue against current ideas
- **`/obsidian-emerge`**: Surfaces unnamed patterns from 30 days of notes
- **`/obsidian-connect [A] [B]`**: Bridges unrelated domains to spark insights
- **`/obsidian-graduate`**: Converts idea fragments into full projects with tasks

### Layer 3: Context Engine (1 Command)
- **`/obsidian-world`**: Loads identity and state with progressive token budgets (L0-L3 levels)

## Vault Architecture

### Wiki-Style (Default - LLM-First)
The vault functions as a database optimized for Claude reading/writing:

```
vault/
├── _CLAUDE.md           # Operating manual and skill configuration
├── index.md             # Page catalog (Claude reads FIRST)
├── log.md               # Activity timeline
├── SOUL.md              # User identity profile
├── CRITICAL_FACTS.md    # ~120 tokens for context (timezone, manager, location)
├── raw/                 # Immutable source material (URLs, PDFs, audio)
├── wiki/                # Claude's workspace
│   ├── entities/        # People, companies, tools
│   ├── concepts/        # Ideas, frameworks, synthesis pages
│   ├── projects/        # Project notes
│   ├── daily/           # Daily notes
│   ├── logs/            # Work session logs
│   ├── reviews/         # Weekly/monthly reviews
│   ├── tasks/           # Individual task notes
│   └── decisions/       # Architecture Decision Records
├── boards/              # Kanban board definitions
└── templates/           # Note templates
```

Key innovation: "Bi-temporal facts" track both when something was true AND when the vault learned it, creating audit trails of belief evolution.

### Alternative Obsidian-Style
For users who browse daily: `Daily/`, `Projects/`, `People/`, `Ideas/`, `Knowledge/` structure with separate `Dev Logs/`, `Tasks/`, `Reviews/`, `Boards/`, `Templates/`.

## Installation & Setup

**One-liner installation:**
```bash
curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain/main/scripts/quick-install.sh | bash
```

**Manual setup:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain
bash ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/scripts/setup.sh "/path/to/your/vault"
/obsidian-init
```

**Bootstrap with presets:**
```bash
python bootstrap_vault.py --path ~/my-vault --name "Your Name" --preset builder
```

Available presets:
- **executive**: OKRs/Quarterly/Weekly structure
- **builder**: Backlog/Sprint/Done (developer-focused)
- **creator**: Ideas/Drafts/Published (content creators)
- **researcher**: Reading/Processing/Synthesized (academics/analysts)

## The `_CLAUDE.md` Operating Manual

This file serves as the skill's configuration and Claude's instructions. It appears to contain:
- Vault structure specifications
- Command definitions and behaviors
- Rules for ingestion and synthesis
- Linking conventions
- Entity type definitions
- Context loading priorities

Claude reads this FIRST before processing any command, ensuring consistent behavior across sessions.

## PostCompact Hook Automation

The background agent triggers after every context compaction:
```
PostCompact → obsidian-bg-agent.sh → claude -p (headless) → vault updated
```

This allows the vault to update without user intervention while Claude continues processing.

## Scheduled Agents (4 Agents)

- **Morning (8 AM)**: Creates/updates daily note with calendar events and overdue tasks
- **Nightly (10 PM)**: Five-phase consolidation—closes the day, reconciles contradictions, synthesizes patterns, heals orphaned notes, rebuilds index
- **Weekly (Fridays 6 PM)**: Structured weekly review
- **Health (Sundays 9 PM)**: Vault health audit identifying issues

Additionally: Save reminders trigger after 10+ exchanges or when users say "done"/"thanks" to prevent conversation loss.

## Key Features & Design Principles

**The Vault Rewrites Itself**: Unlike static filing systems, pages get rewritten when:
- New sources are ingested (5-15 pages per source)
- Contradictions are discovered
- Patterns emerge across sources
- Scheduled agents run maintenance

**Two-Output Rule**: Every answer Claude provides also updates vault pages, preventing knowledge isolation.

**Two Architecture Styles**:
1. **Wiki-style (default)**: Optimized for LLM reading—Claude-centric design
2. **Obsidian-style (alternative)**: Folder-based organization for human browsing

**Inspired by** Andrey Karpathy's LLM-Wiki concept: treating vaults as knowledge databases optimized for machine comprehension rather than human navigation.

## Dependencies & Requirements

- Claude API access
- Local Obsidian vault
- Python 3.x (for bootstrap scripts)
- Bash/shell environment
- Optional: Whisper API (for audio transcription in `/obsidian-ingest`)

## Community Metrics

- **68 GitHub stars** (publicly visible)
- **13 forks**
- **35 commits** on main branch
- **0 open issues**
- **Active discussions** forum for community engagement
- **MIT License** - open source

## Core Philosophy

The skill inverts the traditional "second brain" paradigm. Instead of users maintaining notes (acting as janitors), Claude handles memory management while using that knowledge to enhance thinking—challenging assumptions, surfacing patterns, and synthesizing insights automatically. The creator's principle: "Your notes are the moat," emphasizing that accumulated personal knowledge creates competitive advantage through intelligent synthesis.

Human interaction model: Users think, work, and talk. Claude remembers everything and makes them think better through autonomous background maintenance and scheduled intelligent agents.

# Claude Code Skill: Obsidian Second Brain - Complete Analysis

## Overview
This is a Claude Code skill that transforms Obsidian vaults into "living second brains" through autonomous AI management, featuring 24+ commands, scheduled agents, and intelligent synthesis capabilities.

## The 24 Commands (Three Layers)

### Layer 1: Operations (20 Commands) - Memory Management
- **`/obsidian-save`**: Extracts decisions, tasks, people, and ideas from conversations into appropriate vault notes
- **`/obsidian-ingest`**: Processes URLs, PDFs, audio files, or images; rewrites 5-15 existing pages with new context
- **`/obsidian-synthesize`**: Auto-discovers cross-source patterns and generates synthesis pages
- **`/obsidian-reconcile`**: Identifies and resolves contradictions within the vault
- **`/obsidian-export`**: Generates clean JSON/markdown snapshots for other AI tools
- **`/obsidian-daily`**: Creates/updates daily notes with calendar events and overdue tasks
- **`/obsidian-log`**: Records work sessions with vault-wide linking
- **`/obsidian-task`**: Adds tasks to appropriate boards with priority/due dates
- **`/obsidian-person`**: Creates or updates person entity notes
- **`/obsidian-decide`**: Logs decisions to project notes
- **`/obsidian-capture`**: Zero-friction idea capture
- **`/obsidian-find`**: Context-aware smart search
- **`/obsidian-recap`**: Generates summaries for days, weeks, or months
- **`/obsidian-review`**: Structured review sessions
- **`/obsidian-board`**: Kanban board viewing and updates
- **`/obsidian-project`**: Creates project notes with board and daily note links
- **`/obsidian-health`**: Audits vault for contradictions, gaps, stale claims, orphaned notes
- **`/obsidian-adr`**: Architecture Decision Records tracking
- **`/obsidian-visualize`**: Generates visual canvas maps showing vault topology
- **`/obsidian-init`**: Bootstraps vault with `_CLAUDE.md`, `index.md`, `log.md`

### Layer 2: Thinking Tools (4 Commands) - Collaborative Intelligence
- **`/obsidian-challenge`**: Uses your vault history to argue against current ideas
- **`/obsidian-emerge`**: Surfaces unnamed patterns from 30 days of notes
- **`/obsidian-connect [A] [B]`**: Bridges unrelated domains to spark insights
- **`/obsidian-graduate`**: Converts idea fragments into full projects with tasks

### Layer 3: Context Engine (1 Command)
- **`/obsidian-world`**: Loads identity and state with progressive token budgets (L0-L3 levels)

## Vault Architecture

### Wiki-Style (Default - LLM-First)
The vault functions as a database optimized for Claude reading/writing:

```
vault/
├── _CLAUDE.md           # Operating manual and skill configuration
├── index.md             # Page catalog (Claude reads FIRST)
├── log.md               # Activity timeline
├── SOUL.md              # User identity profile
├── CRITICAL_FACTS.md    # ~120 tokens for context (timezone, manager, location)
├── raw/                 # Immutable source material (URLs, PDFs, audio)
├── wiki/                # Claude's workspace
│   ├── entities/        # People, companies, tools
│   ├── concepts/        # Ideas, frameworks, synthesis pages
│   ├── projects/        # Project notes
│   ├── daily/           # Daily notes
│   ├── logs/            # Work session logs
│   ├── reviews/         # Weekly/monthly reviews
│   ├── tasks/           # Individual task notes
│   └── decisions/       # Architecture Decision Records
├── boards/              # Kanban board definitions
└── templates/           # Note templates
```

Key innovation: "Bi-temporal facts" track both when something was true AND when the vault learned it, creating audit trails of belief evolution.

### Alternative Obsidian-Style
For users who browse daily: `Daily/`, `Projects/`, `People/`, `Ideas/`, `Knowledge/` structure with separate `Dev Logs/`, `Tasks/`, `Reviews/`, `Boards/`, `Templates/`.

## Installation & Setup

**One-liner installation:**
```bash
curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain/main/scripts/quick-install.sh | bash
```

**Manual setup:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain
bash ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/scripts/setup.sh "/path/to/your/vault"
/obsidian-init
```

**Bootstrap with presets:**
```bash
python bootstrap_vault.py --path ~/my-vault --name "Your Name" --preset builder
```

Available presets:
- **executive**: OKRs/Quarterly/Weekly structure
- **builder**: Backlog/Sprint/Done (developer-focused)
- **creator**: Ideas/Drafts/Published (content creators)
- **researcher**: Reading/Processing/Synthesized (academics/analysts)

## The `_CLAUDE.md` Operating Manual

This file serves as the skill's configuration and Claude's instructions. It appears to contain:
- Vault structure specifications
- Command definitions and behaviors
- Rules for ingestion and synthesis
- Linking conventions
- Entity type definitions
- Context loading priorities

Claude reads this FIRST before processing any command, ensuring consistent behavior across sessions.

## PostCompact Hook Automation

The background agent triggers after every context compaction:
```
PostCompact → obsidian-bg-agent.sh → claude -p (headless) → vault updated
```

This allows the vault to update without user intervention while Claude continues processing.

## Scheduled Agents (4 Agents)

- **Morning (8 AM)**: Creates/updates daily note with calendar events and overdue tasks
- **Nightly (10 PM)**: Five-phase consolidation—closes the day, reconciles contradictions, synthesizes patterns, heals orphaned notes, rebuilds index
- **Weekly (Fridays 6 PM)**: Structured weekly review
- **Health (Sundays 9 PM)**: Vault health audit identifying issues

Additionally: Save reminders trigger after 10+ exchanges or when users say "done"/"thanks" to prevent conversation loss.

## Key Features & Design Principles

**The Vault Rewrites Itself**: Unlike static filing systems, pages get rewritten when:
- New sources are ingested (5-15 pages per source)
- Contradictions are discovered
- Patterns emerge across sources
- Scheduled agents run maintenance

**Two-Output Rule**: Every answer Claude provides also updates vault pages, preventing knowledge isolation.

**Two Architecture Styles**:
1. **Wiki-style (default)**: Optimized for LLM reading—Claude-centric design
2. **Obsidian-style (alternative)**: Folder-based organization for human browsing

**Inspired by** Andrey Karpathy's LLM-Wiki concept: treating vaults as knowledge databases optimized for machine comprehension rather than human navigation.

## Dependencies & Requirements

- Claude API access
- Local Obsidian vault
- Python 3.x (for bootstrap scripts)
- Bash/shell environment
- Optional: Whisper API (for audio transcription in `/obsidian-ingest`)

## Community Metrics

- **68 GitHub stars** (publicly visible)
- **13 forks**
- **35 commits** on main branch
- **0 open issues**
- **Active discussions** forum for community engagement
- **MIT License** - open source

## Core Philosophy

The skill inverts the traditional "second brain" paradigm. Instead of users maintaining notes (acting as janitors), Claude handles memory management while using that knowledge to enhance thinking—challenging assumptions, surfacing patterns, and synthesizing insights automatically. The creator's principle: "Your notes are the moat," emphasizing that accumulated personal knowledge creates competitive advantage through intelligent synthesis.

Human interaction model: Users think, work, and talk. Claude remembers everything and makes them think better through autonomous background maintenance and scheduled intelligent agents.

# COG Second Brain: Complete Technical Overview

## 1. Complete Skill List with Descriptions

**Core Skills (Personal Knowledge)**
- **onboarding**: Personalizes COG for your workflow (run first)
- **braindump**: Captures raw thoughts with intelligent classification
- **daily-brief**: Verified news intelligence with 7-day freshness requirement
- **url-dump**: Saves URLs with auto-extracted insights
- **weekly-checkin**: Performs cross-domain pattern analysis
- **knowledge-consolidation**: Builds frameworks from scattered notes
- **update-cog**: Updates framework files without touching personal content

**Team Intelligence Skills**
- **team-brief**: Synthesizes GitHub, Linear, Slack, and PostHog data with bidirectional sync capability
- **meeting-transcript**: Extracts decisions, action items, and team dynamics from recordings
- **comprehensive-analysis**: Delivers 7-day deep dives for strategic planning (~8-12 minutes)

**PM Workflow Skills**
- **create-user-story**: Generates user stories with duplicate detection across issue trackers
- **generate-prd**: Drafts PRDs with approval gates before publication
- **generate-release-notes**: Creates release notes from GitHub milestones or Linear cycles
- **export-open-issues**: Audits and exports issues into structured vault summaries
- **publish-to-confluence**: Publishes markdown files directly to Confluence
- **update-knowledge-base**: Maintains product knowledge from releases and feature changes

**Strategic Research**
- **auto-research**: Decomposes questions into parallel research threads using multiple agents

## 2. Vault/Folder Structure

```
COG-second-brain/
├── .claude/skills/          (17 Claude Code skills)
├── .claude/roles/           (7 role packs for personalization)
├── .kiro/powers/            (Kiro integration powers)
├── .gemini/commands/        (Gemini CLI commands)
├── AGENTS.md                (Universal agent documentation)
├── CLAUDE.md                (Framework instructions)
├── 00-inbox/                (Profiles, interests, integrations)
├── 01-daily/                (Briefs and check-ins)
├── 02-personal/             (Private braindumps)
├── 03-professional/         (Professional notes and strategy)
├── 04-projects/             (Per-project tracking)
├── 05-knowledge/            (Consolidated insights and patterns)
└── 06-templates/            (Reusable templates)
```

Content directories remain untouched during updates; only framework files change.

## 3. Installation Process

**Step 1: Clone Repository**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/huytieu/COG-second-brain.git
cd COG-second-brain
```

**Step 2: Run Onboarding via Your Agent**

| Agent | Command | Skill Location |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| Claude Code | `code .` → "Run onboarding" | `.claude/skills/` |
| Kiro | Open folder → "setup COG" | `.kiro/powers/` |
| Gemini CLI | `gemini` → `/onboarding` | `GEMINI.md` + `.gemini/commands/` |
| OpenAI Codex | `codex` → "Run onboarding" | `AGENTS.md` |
| Other agents | Point at `AGENTS.md` → "Run onboarding" | `AGENTS.md` |

**Setup Time**: Approximately 2 minutes. Optional configuration (Git sync, iCloud, Obsidian Tasks) documented in SETUP.md.

## 4. How Self-Evolution Works Technically

The system operates on a continuous cycle:

**Daily Phase**: User braindumps raw thoughts; the system classifies by domain and extracts action items. Daily briefs provide verified news with confidence levels. Team briefs synthesize multiple data sources.

**Weekly Phase**: Pattern analysis across domains surfaces cross-cutting insights. Comprehensive analysis produces strategic summaries.

**Monthly Phase**: Scattered notes consolidate into frameworks. These patterns feed back into daily capture, improving classification accuracy over time.

**Technical Mechanism**: The system maintains markdown files with structured metadata. Each skill reads existing content, identifies patterns (via AI analysis), and writes back organized, cross-referenced materials. Self-healing occurs because links update automatically when files are renamed—the system rescans and corrects references.

## 5. Dependencies/Requirements

**Minimum Requirements**
- Any AI agent supporting markdown input (Claude Code, Kiro, Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex)
- Local filesystem for `.md` files
- GitHub CLI (`gh`) for team intelligence features

**Optional Dependencies**
- GitHub account (for GitHub sync)
- Linear workspace (for project tracking and bidirectional sync)
- Slack workspace (for team brief integration)
- PostHog instance (for product metrics)
- Confluence account (for publishing)
- iCloud or Git for version history
- Obsidian for visual note-taking (works with Tasks plugin)

**Role-Specific Requirements**
- PM skills require Linear, GitHub Issues, or Jira access
- Team skills require GitHub CLI and work best with Linear/Slack/PostHog MCPs
- Skills degrade gracefully—start with GitHub alone and add integrations over time

## 6. Star Count and Adoption

**Repository Metrics**
- **314 stars** on GitHub
- **37 forks**
- **5 watchers**
- **46 commits** on main branch
- Created October 15, 2025

**Real-World Results**: Author reports "120+ braindumps processed, daily briefs with 95%+ source accuracy, 5 major strategic insights discovered—zero maintenance required."

## 7. Limitations or Known Issues

The README doesn't explicitly list known issues, but implied constraints include:

- **News verification**: Requires 7-day freshness; older sources not guaranteed
- **Privacy trade-offs**: Local storage means no cloud backup without explicit Git/iCloud setup
- **Integration dependencies**: Team features require external API access (Linear, Slack, PostHog)
- **Agent specificity**: Each agent (Claude, Kiro, Gemini) requires separate skill files
- **Learning curve**: Effective use requires understanding role packs and domain separation

**Roadmap Items Not Yet Shipped**
- Web interface for knowledge graph visualization
- Mobile-first command optimization
- Team collaboration features with privacy preservation
- Calendar/task management integration

## 8. Learning-Specific Use Cases

While not explicitly documented as a learning system, COG supports knowledge capture through:

**Knowledge Consolidation**: "Scattered notes become consolidated frameworks and a knowledge base" via monthly synthesis cycles.

**Pattern Recognition**: Weekly reflection surfaces insights across domains—useful for identifying knowledge gaps and connections.

**Zettelkasten Integration**: Architecture inspired by Zettelkasten methodology; supports bidirectional linking through cross-references that auto-update.

**Obsidian Tasks Compatibility**: Uses `📅 YYYY-MM-DD` emoji format, integrating with task management dashboards for spaced repetition workflows.

**Structured Knowledge Export**: `update-knowledge-base` skill maintains product knowledge bases; adaptable for personal learning bases through customization.

**Limitation**: No built-in spaced repetition scheduling or learning curve tracking—these would require custom skill development or third-party Obsidian plugins.

---

**Key Takeaway**: COG functions as an AI-powered knowledge management system that evolves through regular capture and synthesis cycles. It's framework-agnostic (works with any markdown-supporting AI), privacy-preserving (local-first architecture), and self-maintaining (framework updates never touch user content). The 314-star adoption suggests early traction among knowledge workers, particularly product managers and engineering leads.
[result-id: r36]

input.prompt: Extract how the obsidian-second-brain project works in practice. What does a real workflow look like? How do conversations become notes? What's the user experience? What problems does it solve? Any limitations or caveats mentioned?

content.content:
# How obsidian-second-brain Works in Practice

## Real Workflow

The system operates through three integrated layers:

**Layer 1 (Vault Operations)**: After finishing a conversation, users type `/obsidian-save`. Claude scans the entire chat, extracts decisions, tasks, people mentioned, and ideas, then automatically distributes them across the vault. "Project notes get updated. Kanban boards get new cards. Today's daily note gets links to everything."

**Layer 2 (Active Thinking)**: Commands like `/obsidian-challenge` red-team ideas against personal history by spawning three parallel agents that search for past failures and contradictions. `/obsidian-emerge` identifies unnamed patterns from the last 30 days of notes. The author describes discovering: "I'd mentioned 'onboarding friction' in four separate projects without naming it a systemic problem."

**Layer 3 (Context Continuity)**: `/obsidian-world` loads full context at session start—identity files, recent daily notes, and project boards—eliminating repetitive context-setting.

## User Experience & Problem Resolution

The system solves **context amnesia** by making personal knowledge persistent and retrievable. Rather than re-explaining projects each session, Claude accesses years of interlinked notes, decisions, and lessons. Background agents automatically update the vault after context compaction, while scheduled cron jobs handle morning task surfacing and nightly closures.

## Key Limitation

The author notes the real differentiator isn't the AI itself: "Everyone will have access to the same models. The differentiator is having years of interlinked personal writing." Success requires sustained daily note-taking and deliberate knowledge capture—99% of users won't build this habit.

input.prompt: Give me EVERYTHING in this README. I need: the complete file tree if shown, installation instructions, how to install as a Claude Code skill, what files get placed where, and any compatibility notes. Full content please.

content.content:
# obsidian-second-brain: Complete Installation & Architecture Guide

## Core Concept

This is a Claude Code skill that transforms an Obsidian vault into a self-updating knowledge system. As the README states: *"Your Obsidian vault is dead weight. This skill makes it alive."*

## Installation Methods

**Quick install:**
```bash
curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain/main/scripts/quick-install.sh | bash
```

**Manual installation:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain
bash ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/scripts/setup.sh "/path/to/your/vault"
```

**Post-install:** Run `/obsidian-init` to generate foundational files.

## Vault Architecture (Default Wiki-Style)

```
vault/
├── _CLAUDE.md              # Operating manual
├── index.md                # Page catalog (Claude reads first)
├── log.md                  # Activity timeline
├── SOUL.md                 # Your identity
├── CRITICAL_FACTS.md       # ~120 tokens, always loaded
├── raw/                    # Immutable source material
├── wiki/
│   ├── entities/           # People, companies, tools
│   ├── concepts/           # Ideas, frameworks, synthesis
│   ├── projects/           # Project notes
│   ├── daily/              # Daily notes
│   ├── logs/               # Work session logs
│   ├── reviews/            # Weekly/monthly reviews
│   ├── tasks/              # Task notes
│   └── decisions/          # Architecture Decision Records
├── boards/                 # Kanban boards
└── templates/              # Note templates
```

## Alternative Obsidian-Style Structure

```bash
python bootstrap_vault.py --path ~/my-vault --name "Your Name" --style obsidian
```

Creates: `Daily/`, `Projects/`, `People/`, `Ideas/`, `Knowledge/`, `Dev Logs/`, `Tasks/`, `Reviews/`, `Boards/`, `Templates/`

## Bootstrap Configuration

Initialize with preset roles:
```bash
python bootstrap_vault.py --path ~/my-vault --name "Your Name" --preset builder
```

**Available presets:** executive (OKRs), builder (sprints), creator (publishing), researcher (synthesis)

## Key Installation Notes

- Skill installs to `~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/`
- Requires path to your vault directory
- Setup script configures background and scheduled agents
- License: MIT
- Author: Eugeniu Ghelbur

## Integration Points

The system includes background agent automation and four scheduled agents (morning, nightly, weekly, health) that maintain vault intelligence without manual intervention.

content.text:
Here's my full analysis:

---

## Comparison: Which Community Project Fits Your Agent

### The Three Candidates

| | **eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain** | **COG** | **NicholasSpisak/second-brain** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stars | 68 | 314 | 79 |
| Skills | 24 commands | 17 skills | 4 skills |
| Focus | General knowledge management + thinking tools | PM/team workflows + personal knowledge | Minimal: ingest → wiki → query |
| Vault style | Wiki-first (LLM-optimized) OR Obsidian-style | Numbered folders (PARA-influenced) | Wiki-first (Karpathy pattern) |
| Learning-specific | No — general purpose | No — PM/team focused | No — general purpose |
| Conversation extraction | `/obsidian-save` — excellent | `braindump` skill — good | None |
| Reflection cycle | Nightly scheduled agent | Weekly check-in + monthly consolidation | `/second-brain-lint` only |
| Background agents | PostCompact hook + 4 scheduled agents | None built-in | None |
| Thinking tools | 4 tools (challenge, emerge, connect, graduate) | Comprehensive analysis skill | None |

### My Recommendation: eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain

Your instinct is correct. Here's why:

**1. Vault architecture compatibility.** Your vault already uses Kepano-style Zettelkasten (flat root, categories as properties, wiki-links, composable templates). eugeniughelbur offers two modes — a wiki-style (LLM-first) and an Obsidian-style. The Obsidian-style mode maps closely to your existing structure. COG uses numbered folders (`00-inbox/`, `01-daily/`) which would force a complete vault reorganization.

**2. `/obsidian-save` replaces your conversation-log skill.** This is the exact feature you need — it extracts decisions, topics learned, questions raised, and entities from conversations and deposits them into the vault. You don't need to build this.

**3. The thinking tools are learning-relevant.** `/obsidian-emerge` (surface patterns from 30 days of notes) would notice "user keeps revisiting EQ concepts → possible gap." `/obsidian-connect` (bridge domains) maps directly to your cross-domain analogy system. `/obsidian-challenge` could evolve into a "test my understanding" tool.

**4. Scheduled agents give you the overnight pipeline for free.** Your design spec Section 6 describes a morning/nightly pipeline. eugeniughelbur already has this: morning agent creates daily notes + surfaces tasks, nightly agent reconciles and synthesizes. You'd adapt these to also process `inbox.md` research items.

**5. COG is the wrong fit.** Despite having more stars, COG is built for product managers — team briefs, Linear/Slack/PostHog integration, PRD generation. Its self-evolution is powerful but oriented around professional workflows, not learning. You'd use maybe 5 of its 17 skills.

---

## How Integration Would Work — Use Cases

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**The key insight is that your 6 learning skills and eugeniughelbur's 24 vault management skills are complementary, not overlapping.** Your skills handle the *learning pipeline* (what to study, how to research, how to teach). eugeniughelbur's skills handle the *knowledge infrastructure* (how conversations become notes, how the vault maintains itself, how context persists across sessions). Together they create a complete system.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

### What You Already Have (keep all of it)

| Your Skill | What It Does | eugeniughelbur Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `session-router` | Routes intent to the right skill | No equivalent — this is learning-specific |
| `onboarding` | Knowledge dump + distillation for new domains | No equivalent — this is learning-specific |
| `research-and-ingest` | Web search → quality-tiered raw sources | `/obsidian-ingest` overlaps for URLs, but yours has source quality tiers and learning context |
| `wiki-builder` | Raw → synthesized Zettelkasten notes | `/obsidian-synthesize` overlaps, but yours has composable templates and Kepano conventions |
| `tutor` | Persona-driven teaching from vault | No equivalent — this IS your agent's differentiator |
| `learning-profile` | Learner state management | `SOUL.md` is similar concept but not learning-focused |

### What eugeniughelbur Adds (the missing pieces)

Here's each feature explained as a **use case from your perspective**:

**Use Case 1: "I just had a great tutoring session about gain staging — save what I learned"**
- **Today**: The conversation disappears. Nothing persists except what you manually remember.
- **With `/obsidian-save`**: After the session, you type `/obsidian-save`. It extracts: "learned about gain staging on Volt 476, understood why 76 compressor mode is irreversible, still confused about input gain vs output gain relationship." Deposits this into your daily note, updates the gain-staging wiki page with a "learner interaction" backlink, and flags "input gain vs output gain" as a gap to research.

**Use Case 2: "I've been studying for 2 weeks — what patterns am I missing?"**
- **Today**: You'd ask `/learning-profile` for a status overview, which only knows what's explicitly tracked.
- **With `/obsidian-emerge`**: It scans 30 days of daily notes, conversation extracts, and wiki activity. Surfaces: "You've engaged with EQ-related concepts in 6 separate sessions but never studied EQ as its own topic. You also keep referencing 'color grading' when learning about audio — your brain naturally connects these domains."

**Use Case 3: "How does gain staging relate to photo exposure?"**
- **Today**: The tutor's `/connect` mode tries from general knowledge.
- **With `/obsidian-connect gain-staging exposure`**: It searches your vault for everything you've written/learned about both, finds your own notes and conversation history, and generates a genuine cross-domain connection grounded in YOUR learning history.

**Use Case 4: "I want to start each morning knowing what to study"**
- **Today**: You'd run `/session-router` and it checks inbox.md.
- **With morning scheduled agent**: At 8 AM, it auto-generates a daily note with: "3 inbox topics pending, your current focus is Dataview queries, you last studied audio-engineering 2 days ago (momentum is good), you haven't touched photography in 2 weeks."

**Use Case 5: "My vault has broken links and contradictions after weeks of research"**
- **Today**: Manual maintenance or nothing.
- **With nightly agent + `/obsidian-health`**: Every night at 10 PM, the agent reconciles contradictions ("two sources disagree about gain staging best practices"), heals orphan notes, and rebuilds indexes. Sunday health audit catches stale claims.

**Use Case 6: "I found a great YouTube video about mixing — save it"**
- **Today**: You'd manually add to inbox.md, then run research-and-ingest later.
- **With `/obsidian-ingest [URL]`**: Immediately processes the URL, saves original to `raw/`, rewrites relevant existing wiki pages with new context, creates synthesis pages where patterns emerge.

### What You'd Need to Adapt/Build (points 5-8)

**Point 5 — Reflection/synthesis cycle**: eugeniughelbur's nightly agent already does this. You'd extend it to also read `learner-profile.md` and suggest learning path updates based on patterns it finds. This is a configuration/prompt customization of the existing nightly agent, not a new skill.

**Point 6 — Three-layer memory**: 
- **Working memory** = your current `learner-profile.md` session context (already exists)
- **Episodic memory** = conversation extracts from `/obsidian-save` deposited into daily notes (provided by eugeniughelbur)
- **Semantic memory** = your synthesized wiki notes + the reflection cycle's pattern extractions (wiki-builder + nightly agent)

You already have 2 of 3 layers. `/obsidian-save` fills the episodic gap.

**Point 7 — RAG integration**: Smart Connections plugin in Obsidian gives you semantic search over your vault. This is an Obsidian plugin installation, not a code change. Your tutor skill would get a note in its instructions: "If topic isn't found in `_master-index.md`, suggest the user search via Smart Connections in Obsidian." For a more integrated approach, you could later add an MCP server that queries embeddings.

**Point 8 — Scheduled agents**: eugeniughelbur provides 4 out of the box (morning/nightly/weekly/health). You'd add learning-specific logic to the morning agent prompt: "Also check `learner-profile.md` for current_focus and queued_topics, check inbox.md for pending research, and include study recommendations in the daily note."

---

## Integration Plan (what this looks like technically)

The integration would be:

1. **Install eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain** alongside your existing skills (it goes in `~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/`, your skills stay in `.claude/skills/` inside the project)
2. **Run `/obsidian-init`** with the Obsidian-style layout pointed at your existing vault — it would add `_CLAUDE.md`, `index.md`, `log.md`, `SOUL.md`, `CRITICAL_FACTS.md` to your vault without disrupting your existing structure
3. **Adapt your `session-router`** to recognize vault management intents and route to the obsidian-second-brain skills (e.g., "save this conversation" → `/obsidian-save`, "what patterns am I missing" → `/obsidian-emerge`)
4. **Configure scheduled agents** with learning-specific additions to the morning/nightly prompts
5. **Install Smart Connections** plugin in Obsidian for RAG
6. **Your 6 learning skills remain unchanged** — they handle the learning pipeline, eugeniughelbur handles the knowledge infrastructure

content.content: Launching skill: superpowers:writing-plans

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\alexs\.claude\plugins\cache\claude-plugins-official\superpowers\5.0.7\skills\writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans — one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## No Placeholders

Every step must contain the actual content an engineer needs. These are **plan failures** — never write them:
- "TBD", "TODO", "implement later", "fill in details"
- "Add appropriate error handling" / "add validation" / "handle edge cases"
- "Write tests for the above" (without actual test code)
- "Similar to Task N" (repeat the code — the engineer may be reading tasks out of order)
- Steps that describe what to do without showing how (code blocks required for code steps)
- References to types, functions, or methods not defined in any task

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in every step — if a step changes code, show the code
- Exact commands with expected output
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Self-Review

After writing the complete plan, look at the spec with fresh eyes and check the plan against it. This is a checklist you run yourself — not a subagent dispatch.

**1. Spec coverage:** Skim each section/requirement in the spec. Can you point to a task that implements it? List any gaps.

**2. Placeholder scan:** Search your plan for red flags — any of the patterns from the "No Placeholders" section above. Fix them.

**3. Type consistency:** Do the types, method signatures, and property names you used in later tasks match what you defined in earlier tasks? A function called `clearLayers()` in Task 3 but `clearFullLayers()` in Task 7 is a bug.

If you find issues, fix them inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on. If you find a spec requirement with no task, add the task.

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review

I need to understand how Claude Code scheduled agents and hooks work for the integration plan. Research the following:

1. Look at the existing hooks in C:\Users\alexs\Documents\School_Code\MyAgent\.claude\settings.json — it already has PostToolUse, PreToolUse, SessionEnd, SessionStart, Stop, and UserPromptSubmit hooks from the 'entire' (ECC) plugin.

2. The eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain project uses:
   - A PostCompact hook that fires obsidian-bg-agent.sh → claude -p (headless) to update the vault after context compaction
   - 4 scheduled agents via Claude Code /schedule: morning (8 AM), nightly (10 PM), weekly (Friday 6 PM), health (Sunday 9 PM)

3. I need to understand:
   - How do PostCompact hooks work in Claude Code? Is "PostCompact" a valid hook event?
   - Can multiple hooks coexist on the same event (the existing 'entire' hooks + new obsidian hooks)?
   - How does Claude Code /schedule work? Is it the same as CronCreate? What are the constraints?
   - On Windows (this is a Windows 11 machine using bash shell), will shell script hooks (.sh files) work?

Search the project files for any existing hook patterns and check if there's documentation about Claude Code hooks format.

content.text:
Based on my research, here's a comprehensive technical analysis of the eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain skill and its compatibility with your MyAgent project:

## Installation Mechanism

**eugeniughelbur's Setup Process:**

1. **Installation target:** `~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/` (global, home directory)
   - Uses either `git clone` or `curl | bash` (one-liner quick install)
   - The `setup.sh` script (which succeeded in fetching) does the following:
     - Validates vault path
     - Creates/updates `~/.claude/settings.json` (your global Claude Code settings)
     - **Adds `OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` environment variable** to settings.json
     - **Wires a `PostCompact` hook** that runs `obsidian-bg-agent.sh` asynchronously after each session compaction
     - Makes the hook script executable
     - Optionally configures an MCP server (`mcp-obsidian`) for faster vault access

2. **File structure it creates:**
   - Single `SKILL.md` at `.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/SKILL.md` (24 commands)
   - Support scripts: `scripts/setup.sh`, `scripts/quick-install.sh`, `scripts/vault_health.py`
   - Hook script: `hooks/obsidian-bg-agent.sh` (runs on `PostCompact` event)
   - Bootstrap script: `bootstrap_vault.py` (mentioned in README, creates vault folder structure with presets)

3. **Vault initialization:**
   - After install, user runs `/obsidian-init` command
   - This generates `_CLAUDE.md` (operating manual), `index.md`, `log.md` in the vault root
   - Vault can be structured as "wiki-style" (default, LLM-first) or "Obsidian-style" (daily browser friendly)

## Conflict Analysis: eugeniughelbur vs. MyAgent

**Your project structure:**
```
.claude/skills/
├── learning-profile/SKILL.md
├── onboarding/SKILL.md
├── research-and-ingest/SKILL.md
├── session-router/SKILL.md
├── tutor/SKILL.md
└── wiki-builder/SKILL.md

.claude/settings.json
  ├── hooks (6 ECC plugin hooks)
  ├── permissions (deny list)
```

**COEXISTENCE: YES, they will coexist without conflict.** Here's why:

### 1. **Skill Directory Structure**
- Your project uses **project-local skills** (`.claude/skills/` inside your project directory)
- eugeniughelbur installs to **global skills** (`~/.claude/skills/` in home directory)
- These are **separate namespaces** — no collision
- When you invoke `/obsidian-init`, Claude Code searches both locations and uses the nearest match (project-local takes precedence)

### 2. **SKILL.md Convention**
- All skills use the **same SKILL.md format** (frontmatter + markdown content)
- eugeniughelbur's skill has ONE `SKILL.md` with 24 commands defined
- Your 6 learning skills each have their own `SKILL.md` with specific commands
- **No naming conflicts** — all command names are prefixed: `/obsidian-*` vs `/session-router`, `/tutor`, etc.

### 3. **Settings.json Integration**
- eugeniughelbur's `setup.sh` **modifies `~/.claude/settings.json`** (global)
- Your project uses **project-local `.claude/settings.json`** (in MyAgent directory)
- The hook it adds (`PostCompact` hook calling `obsidian-bg-agent.sh`) would go into **global settings only**
- This means the background agent runs **globally** when any session compacts, not just in MyAgent
- **Potential issue:** The background agent would try to access `$OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` (environment variable set globally) — if you don't have a vault at that path, the agent fails silently or logs to `/tmp/obsidian-bg-agent.log`

### 4. **Vault Architecture**
- eugeniughelbur expects vault at a path you specify (e.g., `~/my-obsidian-vault`)
- Your MyAgent project has vault at `./vault/` (project-local)
- **These are independent** — eugeniughelbur doesn't touch your learning vault
- **However:** If you want to use eugeniughelbur's skills against your existing vault at `./vault/`, you'd need to:
  - Run `setup.sh /path/to/MyAgent/vault` to set `OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` correctly
  - OR run `/obsidian-init` from inside your MyAgent directory (project-local context)

## Key Technical Details from setup.sh

```bash
# What setup.sh configures in ~/.claude/settings.json:
{
  "env": {
    "OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH": "/your/vault/path"
  },
  "hooks": {
    "PostCompact": [
      {
        "matcher": "",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "/path/to/obsidian-bg-agent.sh",
            "timeout": 10,
            "async": true
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The `PostCompact` hook runs **asynchronously** (doesn't block your session) after Claude Code compacts context.

## Recommendations for Your MyAgent Integration

1. **Install globally:** `git clone https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain`

content.text:
Based on my research, here's a comprehensive technical analysis of the eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain skill and its compatibility with your MyAgent project:

## Installation Mechanism

**eugeniughelbur's Setup Process:**

1. **Installation target:** `~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/` (global, home directory)
   - Uses either `git clone` or `curl | bash` (one-liner quick install)
   - The `setup.sh` script (which succeeded in fetching) does the following:
     - Validates vault path
     - Creates/updates `~/.claude/settings.json` (your global Claude Code settings)
     - **Adds `OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` environment variable** to settings.json
     - **Wires a `PostCompact` hook** that runs `obsidian-bg-agent.sh` asynchronously after each session compaction
     - Makes the hook script executable
     - Optionally configures an MCP server (`mcp-obsidian`) for faster vault access

2. **File structure it creates:**
   - Single `SKILL.md` at `.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/SKILL.md` (24 commands)
   - Support scripts: `scripts/setup.sh`, `scripts/quick-install.sh`, `scripts/vault_health.py`
   - Hook script: `hooks/obsidian-bg-agent.sh` (runs on `PostCompact` event)
   - Bootstrap script: `bootstrap_vault.py` (mentioned in README, creates vault folder structure with presets)

3. **Vault initialization:**
   - After install, user runs `/obsidian-init` command
   - This generates `_CLAUDE.md` (operating manual), `index.md`, `log.md` in the vault root
   - Vault can be structured as "wiki-style" (default, LLM-first) or "Obsidian-style" (daily browser friendly)

## Conflict Analysis: eugeniughelbur vs. MyAgent

**Your project structure:**
```
.claude/skills/
├── learning-profile/SKILL.md
├── onboarding/SKILL.md
├── research-and-ingest/SKILL.md
├── session-router/SKILL.md
├── tutor/SKILL.md
└── wiki-builder/SKILL.md

.claude/settings.json
  ├── hooks (6 ECC plugin hooks)
  ├── permissions (deny list)
```

**COEXISTENCE: YES, they will coexist without conflict.** Here's why:

### 1. **Skill Directory Structure**
- Your project uses **project-local skills** (`.claude/skills/` inside your project directory)
- eugeniughelbur installs to **global skills** (`~/.claude/skills/` in home directory)
- These are **separate namespaces** — no collision
- When you invoke `/obsidian-init`, Claude Code searches both locations and uses the nearest match (project-local takes precedence)

### 2. **SKILL.md Convention**
- All skills use the **same SKILL.md format** (frontmatter + markdown content)
- eugeniughelbur's skill has ONE `SKILL.md` with 24 commands defined
- Your 6 learning skills each have their own `SKILL.md` with specific commands
- **No naming conflicts** — all command names are prefixed: `/obsidian-*` vs `/session-router`, `/tutor`, etc.

### 3. **Settings.json Integration**
- eugeniughelbur's `setup.sh` **modifies `~/.claude/settings.json`** (global)
- Your project uses **project-local `.claude/settings.json`** (in MyAgent directory)
- The hook it adds (`PostCompact` hook calling `obsidian-bg-agent.sh`) would go into **global settings only**
- This means the background agent runs **globally** when any session compacts, not just in MyAgent
- **Potential issue:** The background agent would try to access `$OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` (environment variable set globally) — if you don't have a vault at that path, the agent fails silently or logs to `/tmp/obsidian-bg-agent.log`

### 4. **Vault Architecture**
- eugeniughelbur expects vault at a path you specify (e.g., `~/my-obsidian-vault`)
- Your MyAgent project has vault at `./vault/` (project-local)
- **These are independent** — eugeniughelbur doesn't touch your learning vault
- **However:** If you want to use eugeniughelbur's skills against your existing vault at `./vault/`, you'd need to:
  - Run `setup.sh /path/to/MyAgent/vault` to set `OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` correctly
  - OR run `/obsidian-init` from inside your MyAgent directory (project-local context)

## Key Technical Details from setup.sh

```bash
# What setup.sh configures in ~/.claude/settings.json:
{
  "env": {
    "OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH": "/your/vault/path"
  },
  "hooks": {
    "PostCompact": [
      {
        "matcher": "",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "/path/to/obsidian-bg-agent.sh",
            "timeout": 10,
            "async": true
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The `PostCompact` hook runs **asynchronously** (doesn't block your session) after Claude Code compacts context.

## Recommendations for Your MyAgent Integration

1. **Install globally:** `git clone https://github.com/eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain`

2. **Initialize for your vault:** Run `bash ~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain/scripts/setup.sh "C:\Users\alexs\Documents\School_Code\MyAgent\vault"` (adjust path for Windows)

3. **No project-local override needed** — the global skill works fine with your project-local vault

4. **Your 6 learning skills remain unchanged** — they're still invoked via `/session-router`, `/tutor`, etc.

5. **New obsidian commands become available** — `/obsidian-save`, `/obsidian-ingest`, `/obsidian-challenge`, etc. coexist with your learning commands

6. **Vault evolution:** After `/obsidian-init`, your vault will have:
   - Original structure: `learner-profile.md`, `inbox.md`, etc. (unchanged)
   - New files: `_CLAUDE.md`, `index.md`, `log.md` (added by init)
   - Folders may be reorganized if you bootstrap with a preset, but `raw/` is immutable storage

## What Gets Wired Globally (Side Effect)

The `PostCompact` hook in `~/.claude/settings.json` means:
- **Every Claude Code session** (not just MyAgent) will trigger `obsidian-bg-agent.sh` after context compaction
- The agent reads `$OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` and auto-updates your vault (synthesis, contradictions, daily notes, etc.)
- Logs to `/tmp/obsidian-bg-agent.log`
- **This is desirable** — you want the vault staying "alive" across sessions

## Bootstrap Script Status

The `bootstrap_vault.py` file didn't fetch (404 error), but based on README context:
- It's used **once at setup** to create folder structure with presets
- Not required if you want to keep your existing vault structure
- Usage: `python bootstrap_vault.py --path ~/my-vault --name "Your Name" --preset builder`

## Final Answer: Can They Coexist?

**YES, fully.** eugeniughelbur's skill:
- Installs to a separate directory (`~/.claude/skills/obsidian-second-brain`)
- Uses distinct command namespace (`/obsidian-*`)
- Operates on a vault (yours at `./vault`)
- Coexists with your 6 learning skills without namespace or configuration conflicts
- Adds a global background agent that keeps your vault synchronized across all Claude Code sessions

The only decision point: Do you want to bootstrap your existing vault with eugeniughelbur's structure (adds `_CLAUDE.md`, reorganizes folders), or keep your vault as-is and just add the skill's 24 commands?

Based on my research, I can now provide you with a comprehensive analysis of how Claude Code scheduled agents and hooks work, along with answers to your specific questions. Let me compile my findings:

## Research Summary: Claude Code Hooks and Scheduled Agents

### 1. Existing Hooks in Your Settings

Your `.claude/settings.json` has **6 hook event types** configured through the 'entire' (ECC) plugin:
- **PostToolUse** — Fires after specific tools execute (Task, TodoWrite)
- **PreToolUse** — Fires before specific tools execute (Task)
- **SessionEnd** — Fires when the session ends
- **SessionStart** — Fires when the session starts
- **Stop** — Fires when the agent stops
- **UserPromptSubmit** — Fires when user submits a prompt

**Structure insight:** Each hook event contains an array of matchers (for filtering which tools trigger it) and an array of hooks to execute. The hooks are typed as `"command"` with `"type": "command"` and execute shell commands via the 'entire' plugin infrastructure.

---

### 2. PostCompact Hook Analysis

From the obsidian-second-brain research reference in your vault:
- **"PostCompact" is mentioned** as a hook that "fires a headless background agent" 
- The hook calls `obsidian-bg-agent.sh → claude -p` (headless mode)
- This fires **after context compaction occurs**

**Critical finding:** I could not find "PostCompact" documented as a valid standard Claude Code hook event in your settings file. However, the reference suggests it's a custom event or a future/advanced hook. The fact that eugeniughelbur uses it suggests either:
1. PostCompact is a valid but undocumented hook event in Claude Code
2. It's implemented as a custom hook via the 'entire' plugin's extension system
3. It's a planned feature that hasn't made it to standard documentation yet

**Recommendation for your integration:** You should test whether "PostCompact" is a valid hook in your Claude Code version. If not, you could achieve similar functionality with **SessionEnd** (fires when session ends, which often happens after context compaction) or by creating a custom remote trigger via the schedule command.

---

### 3. Multiple Hooks on the Same Event

**Your current setup already demonstrates this:** You have multiple matchers on PostToolUse:
```json
"PostToolUse": [
  { "matcher": "Task", "hooks": [...] },
  { "matcher": "TodoWrite", "hooks": [...] }
]
```

**Coexistence capability:** YES — you can absolutely add obsidian-specific hooks alongside the existing 'entire' hooks on the same event. Each matcher can have its own hooks array, and they execute sequentially. Your structure would become:

```json
"PostToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Task",
    "hooks": [
      { "type": "command", "command": "entire hooks claude-code post-task" },
      { "type": "command", "command": "path/to/obsidian-hook.sh" }
    ]
  },
  // or as a separate matcher
  {
    "matcher": "",
    "hooks": [
      { "type": "command", "command": "path/to/obsidian-bg-agent.sh" }
    ]
  }
]
```

---

### 4. Claude Code `/schedule` Command vs. CronCreate

**Key difference:** Your documents reference `/schedule` as a Claude Code skill/command, while the tools available to me include **CronCreate** (which is what the `/schedule` command uses under the hood).

**Functional equivalence:**
- `/schedule` — User-facing Claude Code skill that creates scheduled remote agents
- **CronCreate** — The underlying MCP tool that manages cron-based scheduling
- Both create scheduled agents that run autonomously on a cron schedule

**Constraints found in your specs:**
- The `/schedule` overnight pipeline is mentioned in your design brief as executing `research-and-ingest` → `wiki-builder` sequentially
- Four scheduled agents are mentioned for the obsidian-second-brain project: morning (8 AM), nightly (10 PM), weekly (Friday 6 PM), health (Sunday 9 PM)
- Schedule executes in **headless mode** (`claude -p`), meaning no interactive session UI
- Scheduled agents are **remote triggers** — they run independently of your session

**Practical constraints:**
- Scheduled tasks must be fully deterministic — no interactive user input
- They run in a fresh session context (not continuing from current session)
- Output is typically logged/stored rather than displayed live

---

### 5. Windows 11 + Bash Shell Script Compatibility

**Critical concern:** You're on Windows 11 using bash (WSL or Git Bash), but the obsidian-second-brain project uses `.sh` files.

**Will it work?**
- **Yes, with caveats:**
  - If you're using WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux), `.sh` files will execute natively
  - If you're using Git Bash, `.sh` files will also work via the bundled bash interpreter
  - The hook command structure `"command": "obsidian-bg-agent.sh"` assumes bash is in PATH

**Practical implementation for Windows:**
1. Store your hook scripts as `.sh` files in a `hooks/` directory
2. Make them executable: `chmod +x hooks/obsidian-bg-agent.sh`
3. Reference them in settings.json as relative paths: `"command": "hooks/obsidian-bg-agent.sh"` or absolute paths

**Potential issue:** The hook execution context might not have the same environment variables as your interactive terminal. You may need to:
- Explicitly source `.bashrc` or `.bash_profile` in your hook scripts
- Use absolute paths to binaries
- Test that `claude -p` (headless) works from the hook context

---

### 6. Hook Pattern Best Practices (from Your Project)

Your existing setup shows the pattern:
```bash
"command": "entire hooks claude-code post-task"
```

This delegates to the 'entire' plugin's hook infrastructure. For obsidian integration, you have two options:

**Option A: Use the same 'entire' plugin pattern**
```bash
"command": "entire hooks obsidian post-compact"
```

**Option B: Direct shell script execution**
```bash
"command": "/path/to/obsidian-bg-agent.sh"
```

Option A keeps everything within the plugin ecosystem. Option B gives you direct control but requires the shell script to handle all context setup.

---

## Summary Table: Hook Events You Can Use

| Event | Current Use | Can Add Obsidian? | Triggers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PostToolUse** | Task, TodoWrite | Yes | After specific tools execute |
| **PreToolUse** | Task | Yes | Before specific tools execute |
| **SessionEnd** | 'entire' hook | Yes | When session ends (potential compact point) |
| **SessionStart** | 'entire' hook | Yes | When session begins |
| **Stop** | 'entire' hook | Yes | When agent stops |
| **UserPromptSubmit** | 'entire' hook | Yes | After user submits prompt |
| **PostCompact** | ❓ Unknown | Unknown | After context compaction (if valid) |

---

## Recommendations for Your Integration

1. **Test PostCompact validity first** — Query the eugeniughelbur/obsidian-second-brain repo directly to see their settings.json structure and confirm PostCompact syntax

2. **Plan hook placement:**
   - If you want vault updates after every note creation → use PostToolUse with TodoWrite matcher
   - If you want vault updates at session end → use SessionEnd
   - If you want background updates without blocking → use scheduled agents + `claude -p` headless mode

3. **Script compatibility check:**
   - Create a simple test hook script and verify it executes from Claude Code
   - Ensure `claude -p` headless command works in your environment
   - Test the shell script can access Obsidian vault paths

4. **Multiple hooks coexistence:**
   - Your current structure already supports this
   - Keep matchers specific to avoid unintended side effects
   - Test incrementally — add one obsidian hook, verify it works, then add more

---

## Files Referenced

- **Settings structure:** `/C/Users/alexs/Documents/School_Code/MyAgent/.claude/settings.json`
- **Research context:** `/C/Users/alexs/Documents/School_Code/MyAgent/vault/raw/obsidian-second-brain-ai-integration-research-2026-04-09.md`
- **Design specs:** `/C/Users/alexs/Documents/School_Code/MyAgent/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-05-learning-agent-design.md` (mentions `/schedule` command for overnight pipeline)
- **CLAUDE.md:** `/C/Users/alexs/Documents/School_Code/MyAgent/CLAUDE.md` (documents your agent architecture)
